title: Chapter 45: Blackmoss
content: The facility rose from the industrial wasteland like a fist punched through the earth. Concrete and steel, windowless, bristling with antennae and sensor arrays. Floodlights carved white cones from the darkness. The chain-link fence was topped with razor wire and humming with a current that Wren could hear — a high, angry whine that said *touch me and regret it*.
She crouched in the rain fifty meters from the perimeter and listened.

The data streams here were different from the city. Tighter. More paranoid. Every signal was encrypted, every channel monitored, every node isolated from its neighbors by redundancies within redundancies. Blackmoss didn't trust its own network. That was good — it meant there were gaps in the paranoia, places where one system didn't quite trust another and left tiny seams of vulnerability.
She found the first one in the camera grid.

The outer perimeter cameras were on a thirty-second sweep cycle — each one panning a 180-degree arc before resetting. Their feeds were routed through a central monitoring hub inside the facility, then backed up to a cloud server with a six-second delay. The delay was the vulnerability. If she fuzzed a camera during the gap between its live feed and the backup, the monitors inside would see the cached loop while the camera itself went blind.
She timed it. Counted the sweep. Waited for the camera aimed at the northeast corner to reach the end of its arc — the moment it turned away from the fence section she needed.

She pushed. The camera's LED flickered. Went dark. She had thirty seconds before the sweep brought it back around, and six seconds before the backup caught on. That gave her a twenty-four-second window to cross open ground, breach the fence, and reach the shadow of the generator housing.
She ran.

The rain soaked through her jacket in the first five seconds. Her boots splashed through puddles that reflected the floodlights in fractured orange. The fence loomed ahead — ten feet of chain-link topped with razor wire and threaded with current. She could hear the current singing, and she reached into its frequency and told it to stop.
The hum died. The fence went cold.

She climbed. The chain-link was slick and her fingers ached with the effort of gripping, but the glitch was flowing through her and she moved faster than she should have been able to — fingers finding the links by instinct, feet bracing without thought. She reached the top, grabbed the bar beneath the razor wire, and swung herself over.
Dropped. Landed in a crouch. The impact jarred her knees and she tasted blood where she'd bitten her tongue.
Keep moving.

She pressed herself against the generator housing and listened. The facility's data streams churned around her — security protocols, environmental controls, power distribution. She sorted them quickly. The Hound patrol was on a twelve-minute circuit; she'd heard their coordination signal three minutes ago, which meant she had nine before they came back around. The service entrance on the east side had an electronic lock rated for a Class 3 intrusion attempt. She could override a Class 3 in her sleep.

She moved along the building's edge, staying in the shadows between the floodlights. The concrete was cold and wet and rough against her shoulder. She could smell diesel from the generator, the chemical sharpness of industrial cleaning agents, and beneath it all — faint, disturbing — the sterile medical scent she recognized from the Underlayer.
This place was a cousin to the Underlayer. Smaller. Better hidden. Built for the same purpose.

She reached the service door. Pressed her palm to the lock. Felt the mechanism resist — not a simple magnetic seal this time, but a biometric system designed to read fingerprints and pulse patterns simultaneously. She couldn't fake a fingerprint. But she could make the system *think* it had received a valid read.
She reached into the lock's verification stream. Found the frequency that said *access granted*. Mimicked it. Pushed.
The lock clicked open.

Inside: a corridor lit by flat white panels. The air was conditioned to a precise temperature that made her skin prickle. No visible cameras, but she could hear the surveillance network — a low, pervasive hum that covered every inch of the building. She fuzzed the nearest node and moved.

She navigated by sound. The data streams were her map — she could hear which corridors had traffic, which doors led to occupied rooms, which sections of the facility were pulling the most power. The containment wing was on the lower level, drawing a disproportionate amount of energy for its size. That's where they kept the subjects. That's where they kept Ash.
She found the stairwell. Descended. The temperature dropped two degrees with each floor. By the time she reached the basement level, her breath was visible and her fingers were numb.
The corridor was long and lined with doors. Each door had a small screen beside it displaying a subject number. SUBJECT 15. SUBJECT 17. SUBJECT 19.
They had more of them. More than the thirteen in her files. Her stomach turned.
She followed the vital-sign ping she'd been tracking for days. It led her to a door at the end of the corridor. SUBJECT 13.
Not her number. His. They'd reassigned it — or they'd never used hers for him. Either way, the ping was unmistakable. That thin, reedy signal of a glitch suppressed but not destroyed.
She overrode the lock. The door slid open.
The cell was small. White walls. A metal cot. And on the cot, Ash.

He was on his side, facing the door, but he didn't react when she entered. His eyes were half-closed, unfocused, the pupils dilated beyond what the dim light justified. His tattoos — the glowing geometric patterns she'd traced with her fingers a hundred times — were barely visible. Dim blue lines instead of bright constellations. The suppressant had turned his skin into a sky without stars.

An IV line ran from his arm to a bag of pale fluid on a stand beside the cot. The fluid was barely a quarter full — whatever they were giving him, he was almost through the current dose. That was why the glitch was still there, still fighting. The suppressant was wearing off.

She knelt beside the cot. Her knees hit the cold floor and the sound was loud in the silence. She reached out and touched his face — his cheek was cool, slack, the muscle beneath the skin unresponsive.
"Ash."
A long moment. Then his lips moved. The whisper was barely audible: "I told you not to come."

She leaned closer. Her forehead almost touched his. She could smell the antiseptic on his skin and the faint chemical burn of the suppressant, and beneath those the scent that was just him — smoke and copper and something warm she'd never been able to name.
"When have I ever listened to you?" she said.
His mouth twitched. Not a smile — he didn't have the strength for a smile. But the ghost of one.

She grabbed the IV line and pulled it from his arm. He hissed through his teeth — the first real sound he'd made, the first sign of actual sensation. A thin line of blood welled from the puncture site. She pressed her thumb over it.
"Can you walk?"
"I..." He tried to push himself up. His arms shook. He made it two inches off the cot before his elbows buckled. "No."
"Then I'll carry you."
"You can't—"

"Ash." She said his name like it was the only word she knew. "I broke into a facility that doesn't exist. I crossed a fence that was supposed to electrocute me. I walked past guards and cameras and Hounds and a security system designed to keep people like us in cages. Do you really want to tell me what I can't do?"
He stared at her. And she saw something change in his expression — the fog clearing, the drug releasing its grip, the man surfacing from wherever the suppressant had sent him.
"Okay," he said. His voice was stronger. Barely. "Okay."
She pulled him upright. His arm went over her shoulders and she braced against his weight — heavier than she expected, or maybe she was lighter than she used to be. He swayed. She held.
"I've got you," she said.
"I know." His breath was warm against her ear. "I always knew."

She didn't have time for that. She filed it away — the tenderness in his voice, the trust — and focused on the path ahead. The corridor. The stairwell. The server room she'd identified from the data streams.
Get Ash out? Yes. But first — the core server. The broadcast. The truth.
She adjusted his arm across her shoulders and moved toward the door.
The hallway was still clear. The Hound patrol was five minutes from this wing. The cameras were still fuzzed.
They had five minutes.
Wren started walking.
